narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the admissions model predicted a 64% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which automatically led to the rejection of your law school application. Your profile includes an LSAT score of 37.5, slightly above the typical student's 36.7; an undergraduate GPA of 3.0, a bit below the average 3.21; rankings in the 3rd decile for Year 1 grades and 2nd decile for Year 3, both well under the averages of 5.71 and 5.53; upper-middle family income; male gender; white racial background; and plans to attend full-time. These details paint a picture of solid potential in some areas, like your LSAT, but challenges in consistent academic performance compared to peers. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which strongly nudged the prediction toward expecting bar failure because it was so much lower than typical. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, also low and reinforcing the same concern about early academic struggles. Your LSAT score came next in influence, contributing somewhat toward the failure prediction despite being above average, perhaps because it wasn't standout enough to offset other weaknesses. Family income followed, with your upper-middle bracket playing a modest role in the same direction, then your undergraduate GPA, which being below average added to the risk signal. Less influential were your race, gender, and full-time status, all fairly typical in the dataset and thus not shifting the outcome much. Overall, your lower-than-average school deciles stood out as the top two drivers, putting you below most students who pass the bar on their first try. To boost your future chances, focus on actionable steps like honing study habits and time management to improve grades in any further coursework, as those early deciles suggest room for growth there. Consider retaking the LSAT to push your score even higher, since it had some positive sway but could become a stronger asset. With targeted preparation, you can address these academic patterns and build a profile more aligned with bar-passing success. Keep pushing forward—you have strengths to build on.
